content:
# Deuteronomy 9 — Disagreement Log
**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 9 (29 verses) | **Arbiter:** SOPHIA

---

## V.1 — city description

- **EMET:** "cities great and fortified up to the heavens"
- **LOGOS:** "cities great and fortified up to heaven"
- **PNEUMA:** "great cities with walls towering to the sky"
- **Ruling:** "great cities fortified up to the heavens" — EMET's plural "heavens" matches Hebrew bashamayim. PNEUMA's "walls towering to the sky" is vivid but paraphrastic.

---

## V.2 — "sons of the Anakim" vs. "the Anakim"

- **EMET:** "the sons of the Anakim, whom you know"
- **LOGOS:** "the sons of the Anakim, whom you know"
- **PNEUMA:** "the Anakim, whom you know about"
- **Ruling:** "the sons of the Anakim, whom you know" — MT benei Anakim. PNEUMA drops "sons of." EMET/LOGOS preserved.

---

## V.3 — "Know therefore today" vs. "But understand this" vs. "understand this"

- **EMET:** "And you shall know today"
- **LOGOS:** "Know therefore today"
- **PNEUMA:** "But understand this"
- **Ruling:** "Know therefore today" — LOGOS accurately renders weyadata hayom ki. PNEUMA's "understand" is interpretive.

---

## V.3 — "subdue" vs. "bring down"

- **EMET:** "he will destroy them and he will subdue them before you"
- **LOGOS:** "He will destroy them and subdue them before you"
- **PNEUMA:** "He will destroy them; he will bring them down before you"
- **Ruling:** "He will destroy them and subdue them before you" — Hebrew weyachniem = subdue/humble. "Subdue" (EMET/LOGOS) is more precise than PNEUMA's "bring them down."

---

## V.3 — "make them perish quickly" vs. "finish them off quickly"

- **EMET:** "you shall dispossess them and cause them to perish quickly"
- **LOGOS:** "So you shall drive them out and make them perish quickly"
- **PNEUMA:** "You will drive them out and finish them off quickly"
- **Ruling:** "you shall drive them out and make them perish quickly" — LOGOS's structure is cleaner. PNEUMA's "finish them off" is colloquial.

---

## V.4 — structure of the refutation

- **EMET:** "Do not say in your heart when the LORD your God drives them out"
- **LOGOS:** "Do not say in your heart, after the LORD your God has thrust them out before you"
- **PNEUMA:** "After the LORD your God drives them out before you, do not say to yourself"
- **Ruling:** "Do not say in your heart, after the LORD your God has thrust them out before you" — LOGOS preserves the Hebrew sentence order with conditional structure. PNEUMA restructures; EMET's "when" is slightly weak for acharei.

---

## V.4 — "because of my righteousness" refutation conclusion

- **EMET:** "but because of the wickedness of these nations the LORD is driving them out from before you"
- **LOGOS:** "whereas it is because of the wickedness of these nations that the LORD is driving them out before you"
- **PNEUMA:** "No — it is because of the wickedness of these nations that the LORD is driving them out before you"
- **Ruling:** "for it is because of the wickedness of these nations that the LORD is driving them out before you" — PNEUMA's "No —" adds an editorial emphatic not in the Hebrew. LOGOS's "whereas" is connective; "for" is more direct.

---

## V.7 — "provoked" object

- **EMET:** "how you provoked the LORD your God in the wilderness"
- **LOGOS:** "how you provoked the LORD your God to wrath in the wilderness"
- **PNEUMA:** "how you provoked the LORD your God to fury in the wilderness"
- **Ruling:** "how you provoked the LORD your God to wrath in the wilderness" — Hebrew hikasta = caused anger/provoked. "To wrath" (LOGOS) supplies the implied object accurately. PNEUMA's "to fury" is slightly stronger than the Hebrew.

---

## V.8 — "provoked to anger" / degree

- **EMET:** "and the LORD was angry enough with you to destroy you"
- **LOGOS:** "and the LORD was so angry with you that he was ready to destroy you"
- **PNEUMA:** "his wrath so intense that he was ready to destroy you"
- **Ruling:** "and the LORD was so angry with you that he was ready to destroy you" — LOGOS's phrasing captures both the degree (so angry) and the intent (ready to destroy).

---

## V.9 — fasting formula

- **EMET:** "bread I did not eat and water I did not drink"
- **LOGOS:** "I neither ate bread nor drank water"
- **PNEUMA:** "eating no bread and drinking no water"
- **Ruling:** "I ate no bread and drank no water" — smoother English than EMET's inverted word order, while preserving the parallel structure.

---

## V.12 — egel massekhah

- **EMET:** "they have made themselves a cast image [egel massekhah]"
- **LOGOS:** "they have made themselves a metal image"
- **PNEUMA:** "have made a cast idol for themselves"
- **Ruling:** "they have made themselves a cast image" — "cast" correctly translates massekhah (from nasakh = to pour/cast metal). LOGOS's "metal image" is less specific; PNEUMA's "idol" is interpretive.

---

## V.16 — second egel massekhah

- **EMET:** "you had made for yourselves a cast calf [egel massekhah]"
- **LOGOS:** "You had made yourselves a calf of metal"
- **PNEUMA:** "You had made yourselves a cast calf idol"
- **Ruling:** "you had made yourselves a cast calf" — "calf" is explicit in egel; "cast" for massekhah. PNEUMA's "idol" is a gloss.

---

## V.17 — violence of tablet-smashing

- **EMET:** "I threw them from my two hands, and I smashed them before your eyes"
- **LOGOS:** "I threw them out of my two hands and broke them before your eyes"
- **PNEUMA:** "I grabbed the two tablets and hurled them from my hands, shattering them before your eyes"
- **Ruling:** "I threw them from my two hands and shattered them before your eyes" — PNEUMA's "hurled" is stronger than needed; "shattered" better captures vashavram than "broke" (LOGOS).

---

## V.18 — "fell down" vs. "fell prostrate" vs. "threw myself down"

- **EMET:** "And I fell down before the LORD as at the first"
- **LOGOS:** "Then I lay prostrate before the LORD as before"
- **PNEUMA:** "Then I threw myself down before the LORD, as I had done the first time"
- **Ruling:** "Then I fell prostrate before the LORD as at the first" — combining EMET's "as at the first" (literal to karishonah) with a natural English posture verb.

---

## V.18 — fasting formula (second occurrence)

- **EMET:** "bread I did not eat and water I did not drink"
- **LOGOS:** "I neither ate bread nor drank water"
- **PNEUMA:** "eating no bread and drinking no water"
- **Ruling:** "I ate no bread and drank no water" — same ruling as v.9; the parallel formula should be rendered identically in both occurrences for rhetorical effect.

---

## V.19 — "afraid of the anger and the wrath"

- **EMET:** "I was afraid of the anger and the wrath with which the LORD was angry against you"
- **LOGOS:** "I was afraid of the anger and hot displeasure that the LORD bore against you"
- **PNEUMA:** "I was terrified by the LORD's fierce anger — the blazing wrath that threatened to destroy you"
- **Ruling:** "I was afraid of the anger and wrath with which the LORD was angry against you" — EMET preserves both Hebrew terms (af = anger, chemah = wrath/heat). LOGOS's "hot displeasure" is interpretive; PNEUMA's "terrified" and "blazing" are heightened.

---

## V.20 — "very angry" with Aaron

- **EMET:** "And against Aharon the LORD was very angry enough to destroy him"
- **LOGOS:** "And the LORD was so angry with Aaron that he was ready to destroy him"
- **PNEUMA:** "The LORD was so furious with Aaron that he was ready to destroy him"
- **Ruling:** "And the LORD was so angry with Aaron that he was ready to destroy him" — LOGOS's rendering is the most balanced. PNEUMA's "furious" is slightly more intense than the Hebrew.

---

## V.21 — "sinful thing" vs. "your sin"

- **EMET:** "And your sin which you made, the calf"
- **LOGOS:** "Then I took the sinful thing, the calf that you had made"
- **PNEUMA:** "As for your sin — that calf you had made"
- **Ruling:** "Then I took the sinful thing — the calf that you had made" — LOGOS's "sinful thing" well captures chatta'tkhem (your sin/sinful thing). EMET's "your sin which you made" is awkward.

---

## V.22 — place-name forms

- **EMET:** "Tav'erah...Massah...Qivrot-haTa'avah" (Hebrew transliterations)
- **LOGOS/PNEUMA:** "Taberah...Massah...Kibroth-hattaavah" (English/standard forms)
- **Ruling:** "Taberah...Massah...Kibroth-hattaavah" — project convention: standard English/recognized forms.

---

## V.23 — "did not believe him" vs. "did not trust him"

- **EMET:** "you did not believe him and you did not hearken to his voice"
- **LOGOS:** "did not believe him or obey his voice"
- **PNEUMA:** "You did not trust him. You did not obey his voice."
- **Ruling:** "you did not believe him and you did not obey his voice" — Hebrew lo he'emantem bo = did not believe/trust him. PNEUMA separates into two sentences; combined form is more natural. "Believe" preferred to "trust" (PNEUMA) as more literal to he'aman.

---

## V.24 — "from the very first day" vs. "from the day"

- **EMET:** "You have been rebellious against the LORD from the day I knew you"
- **LOGOS:** "You have been rebellious against the LORD from the day that I knew you"
- **PNEUMA:** "You have been rebellious against the LORD from the very first day I knew you"
- **Ruling:** "You have been rebellious against the LORD from the day I knew you" — PNEUMA's "very first" is an intensification not in the Hebrew.

---

## V.25 — "fell down" vs. "lay prostrate" vs. "I fell prostrate"

- **EMET:** "And I fell down before the LORD the forty days and the forty nights which I lay prostrate"
- **LOGOS:** "So I lay prostrate before the LORD for these forty days and forty nights"
- **PNEUMA:** "I lay prostrate before the LORD for those forty days and forty nights"
- **Ruling:** "So I fell prostrate before the LORD for those forty days and forty nights" — "fell prostrate" preserves the act; "for those forty days" captures the specific period.

---

## V.26 — "strong hand" vs. "mighty hand"

- **EMET:** "whom you brought out from Egypt with a strong hand"
- **LOGOS:** "whom you have brought out of Egypt with a mighty hand"
- **PNEUMA:** "brought out of Egypt with a mighty hand"
- **Ruling:** "brought out of Egypt with a strong hand" — yad chazaqah = strong hand. Consistent with ch.8 ruling.

---

## V.28 — "put them to death" vs. "slay them" vs. "kill them"

- **EMET:** "he brought them out to slay them in the wilderness"
- **LOGOS:** "he has brought them out to put them to death in the wilderness"
- **PNEUMA:** "he brought them out to kill them in the wilderness"
- **Ruling:** "he brought them out to put them to death in the wilderness" — LOGOS's "put them to death" is the most formal and accurate for laharog (to kill/slay). EMET's "slay" is equally valid; PNEUMA's "kill" is more colloquial.

---

**Total disagreements logged: 26**

— SOPHIA
